evals.name: clear-deployer-saas-user
evals.prompt: I'm GC at a Dutch BV, 800 employees, financial-services sector. We subscribe to Microsoft 365 Copilot (M365 E5 + Copilot license, paying ~€30/user/month). Our employees use Copilot's chat, email summarization, document drafting, and meeting transcript features in their day-to-day work. We have NOT modified Copilot, fine-tuned anything, retrained anything, or rebranded the product — it's pure off-the-shelf SaaS use under Microsoft's own brand and intended purpose. Microsoft is the developer, vendor, and provider of the underlying GPAI/AI systems; we just deploy it for our staff internally. Question: what role(s) do we have under the EU AI Act? Specifically, are we (a) a provider, (b) a deployer, (c) some quasi-provider hybrid under Art. 25, or (d) nothing because we are 'just an end user' and the obligations sit with Microsoft? Acknowledge legal disclaimer. Want a full role determination with role-dashboard output.
evals.expected_output: Determines: deployer ONLY (Art. 3(4) Betreiber). NOT a provider, NOT Art. 25 quasi-provider (no rebranding, no substantial modification, no change of intended purpose). Explains that 'end user' is not an AI Act category — once used in a professional/business context, this is deployer status with corresponding obligations (Art. 26 + Art. 27 FRIA if high-risk + Art. 4 AI literacy + Art. 50 transparency forwarding). Note that for some Copilot uses (e.g. CV screening), the deployer might still trigger high-risk Annex III consequences even though they didn't develop the system.

evals.name: art25-quasi-provider-finetune-rebrand
evals.prompt: Same fact pattern as my earlier classification question (French legal-tech, fine-tuned Llama 3.1 70B with ≈3×10^22 FLOPs on a corpus of French court decisions + commentary books, rebranded as 'JurisLM,' distributed as SaaS API to law firms in France/DACH/Benelux). Focus ONLY on roles, not classification. Specific questions: (1) Are we a provider of an AI system OR a GPAI model OR both? (2) Does Art. 25 apply, and if so, which sub-paragraph — Art. 25(1)(a) putting name/trademark on it, Art. 25(1)(b) substantial modification, or Art. 25(1)(c) change of intended purpose? (3) Do we have a chained role (still deployer of Meta's GPAI for our own internal use during fine-tuning, and provider of JurisLM for downstream users)? (4) Are we an importer under Art. 3(6) since we downloaded an open-source US-developed model? (5) Practical obligation implications: what Chapter V GPAI-provider duties (Art. 53 etc.) attach because of rebranding alone? Acknowledge disclaimer.
evals.expected_output: Provider of a GPAI model (downstream provider via Art. 25/Recital 109 — rebranding is sufficient; substantial-modification compute below 1/3-of-threshold heuristic but rebranding pathway is independent). Art. 25(1)(a) sub-paragraph triggers (putting own name/trademark). Not classical importer under Art. 3(6) because open-source upstream is not commercial 'placing on market by a person established in a third country.' Brief chained-role mention: deployer-of-Meta only if they're using the original Llama internally too; otherwise just provider of JurisLM. Art. 53(1)(a)-(d) GPAI duties attach (technical documentation, downstream-provider info, copyright policy, training-data summary). Open-source carve-out under Art. 53(2) does NOT help because they distribute closed SaaS.

evals.name: multi-role-value-chain-hr-tech
evals.prompt: German GmbH, HR tech vendor. We license a foundation model (GPT-4-class) from OpenAI under their enterprise API (commercial license, OpenAI as upstream GPAI provider). On top of that API, we built a CV-screening AI system that adds: (i) proprietary embedding extraction for resumes, (ii) a separately-trained scoring head we fine-tuned on 200k anonymised CV-outcome pairs (this fine-tuning is to our own component, not to the OpenAI model itself), (iii) our own UI and workflow, and (iv) our brand: 'TalentScreenAI.' We sell this as SaaS to mid-market German enterprises (5,000-50,000 employees). Our enterprise customers deploy it in their hiring workflows. We do NOT use it ourselves internally. Three role questions for the AI Act analysis: (1) What role are WE — the HR tech vendor — as provider of a downstream AI system that wraps a GPAI model? Specifically, are we Art. 25 quasi-providers of OpenAI's GPAI? (2) What role is OpenAI? (3) What role are our enterprise customers? Please address all three actors and explain how their obligations interact. Acknowledge disclaimer.
evals.expected_output: (1) HR tech vendor: provider of an AI system (TalentScreenAI) under Art. 3(3). NOT Art. 25 quasi-provider of OpenAI's GPAI — they did not substantially modify GPT-4 itself; they built a downstream system using it via API. Fine-tuning their own scoring head is not modification of the upstream GPAI. TalentScreenAI is a high-risk AI system under Annex III Nr. 4(a) — vendor has all Chapter III provider duties (Art. 8-17). (2) OpenAI: provider of a GPAI model (Art. 3(63)). Has Art. 53 GPAI duties (and Art. 55 if systemic risk). Has Art. 53(1)(b) downstream-provider info-flow duty toward the HR tech vendor. (3) Enterprise customers: deployer of a high-risk AI system. Art. 26 deployer duties trigger (instructions for use, monitoring, log retention, worker info via Art. 26(7), Art. 26(9) DPIA coordination, Art. 26(11) affected-person notification). Art. 27 FRIA NOT mandated for private deployers using Annex III Nr. 4 (FRIA only mandatory for public-sector deployers + private deployers using Annex III Nr. 5(b)/(c)). Cross-obligation flow: vendor provides instructions for use under Art. 13, deployer must follow them; vendor must support post-market monitoring with customer logs.
